sections.title: The Alphabet
sections.desc: 26 letters — same as English, different sounds

items.title: The Dutch alphabet
items.desc: All 26 letters. IJ counts as a single letter in Dutch. Q, X, Y are loanword-only.

sections.title: Vowels
sections.desc: Short/long pairs, digraph vowels, diphthongs

items.title: Short ↔ long vowel families
items.desc: Every Dutch vowel belongs to a family. Short vowels appear in closed syllables (single letter). Long vowels use double letters in closed syllables or single letters in open syllables.

items.title: Digraph vowels: ie, oe, eu
items.desc: Three two-letter vowels that are inherently long. They never get doubled — they are already complete spellings.

items.title: Diphthongs: ei/ij, ui, ou/au
items.desc: Three gliding vowels — each is ONE syllable. Two of them have competing spellings (taught under Irregularities).

items.title: Minimal pairs: short vs long
items.desc: Vowel length changes meaning. Every pair below differs only in the vowel.

items.title: Schwa: the unstressed vowel
items.desc: Schwa /ə/ is the most common vowel in Dutch. Always unstressed. Default spelling: e.

sections.title: 🗣️ Regular & Tricky Consonants
sections.desc: Regular consonants, tricky sounds, and voiced/voiceless pairs

items.title: Consonants: regular
items.desc: These consonants work the same as in English. No surprises.

items.title: Consonants: tricky for English speakers
items.desc: These look familiar but sound different in Dutch.

items.title: Voiced ↔ voiceless pairs
items.desc: Dutch has five voiced/voiceless consonant pairs. At word endings, voiced consonants automatically become voiceless (final devoicing).

sections.title: 🗣️ Digraphs & Clusters
sections.desc: Consonant digraphs (ch, ng, sch), clusters, and the Dutch g

items.title: Consonant digraphs: ch, ng, sch
items.desc: Two (or three) letters that make one sound. Digraphs are NEVER doubled in spelling.

items.title: Consonant clusters
items.desc: Dutch allows complex clusters. Up to 3 consonants at the start (first must be s), up to 4 at the end.

items.title: The Dutch g: regional variants
items.desc: The g/ch sound varies across regions. Northern = hard, throaty. Southern (Brabant, Limburg) = soft, gentle. Both are standard Dutch.

sections.title: ⚙️ Syllable Logic & Spelling
sections.desc: Open vs closed syllables, the core spelling rule, and consonant doubling

items.title: Open vs closed syllables
items.desc: Open syllable = ends in a vowel → long. Closed syllable = ends in a consonant → short. This drives all Dutch vowel spelling.

items.title: Spelling logic: single vs double vowels
items.desc: Three situations, three rules. The syllable type determines how many letters you write.

items.title: Consonant doubling: protecting short vowels
items.desc: Adding -en/-er/-e to a short-vowel word would open the syllable (making the vowel long — wrong). Fix: double the consonant to keep the syllable closed.

sections.title: ⚙️ Advanced Spelling Rules
sections.desc: Drop-one-letter, never-double digraphs, and the decision tree

items.title: Drop one vowel: long vowel + ending
items.desc: When a double-vowel word gets an ending, the syllable opens — so one letter is enough to signal the long vowel.

items.title: Digraphs: never doubled
items.desc: ch and ng are already two letters for one sound. They NEVER get doubled. ie, oe, eu are already two-letter vowels — never write ii, ooe, or euu.

items.title: The decision tree: vowel spelling
items.desc: A step-by-step lookup for any Dutch vowel spelling decision.

cells.rom: does syllable end in vowel or consonant?

cells.ch: write single letter: a, e, o, u
write double letter: aa, ee, oo, uu
write single letter: a, e, i, o, u

sections.title: Stress & Schwa
sections.desc: Word stress rules and the unstressed vowel

items.title: Stress rules
items.desc: Where stress falls in Dutch words.

items.title: Schwa: always unstressed, spelled e
items.desc: Schwa /ə/ never carries stress. Default spelling is e. In casual speech, other unstressed vowels may reduce toward schwa — this is pronunciation only, not a spelling change.
examples: Formal = full vowels, informal = more reduction

sections.title: 🔀 IJ/EI & OU/AU
sections.desc: The competing diphthong pairs: ij vs ei and ou vs au, plus IJ as the 27th letter

items.title: /ɛi/ — two spellings: ij and ei
items.desc: Same sound, two spellings. Distribution is historical — no reliable rule. Learn each word individually.

items.title: IJ — the 27th letter
items.desc: IJ is treated as one letter. Both uppercase when capitalizing: IJsselmeer, not Ijsselmeer. In handwriting often one glyph: ĳ.

items.title: /ɑu/ — two spellings: ou and au
items.desc: Same sound, two spellings. ou is more common; au often in loanwords.

sections.title: 🔀 Devoicing & Kofschip
sections.desc: Final devoicing, v/z alternation, and the 't kofschip rule

items.title: Final devoicing: sounds-like vs spelling
items.desc: At word end, voiced consonants become voiceless. Spelling keeps the underlying form. Find a related word to check.

items.title: V/Z alternation at word boundaries
items.desc: v and z at word start become f and s at word end. Adding an ending restores the original.

items.title: 't Kofschip: past tense -te vs -de
items.desc: Weak verbs: stem ends in t, k, f, s, ch, p → past = -te. Otherwise → -de. Final devoicing can mask the real consonant — always check the stem spelling.

cells.ch: t, k, f, s, ch, p

sections.title: The German Alphabet
sections.desc: 26 letters + Umlauts + Eszett

items.title: German letter grid
items.desc: German uses A-Z plus 4 special characters: Ä, Ö, Ü, ß. The sounds differ from English — tap any letter!

items.title: Pronunciation traps
items.desc: W sounds like English V. V sounds like English F. J sounds like English Y. Z sounds like /ts/. These catch EVERY English speaker.

sections.title: Umlauts & ß
sections.desc: Special characters unique to German

items.title: Ä ä → /ɛ/
items.desc: Like 'e' in 'bed'. The two dots change A into a front vowel.

items.title: Ö ö → /ø/
items.desc: Round your lips for 'oh' and say 'ay'. No English equivalent.

items.title: Ü ü → /y/
items.desc: Round your lips for 'oo' and say 'ee'. No English equivalent.

items.title: ß (Eszett)
items.desc: A sharp S sound /s/. Used after long vowels and diphthongs. Switzerland uses 'ss' instead.

sections.title: Vowel Length
sections.desc: Long vs short changes pronunciation

items.title: Long vowels
items.desc: Doubled vowels (aa, ee, oo), vowels before single consonant, or vowels with silent H.

items.title: Short vowels
items.desc: Before double consonants or consonant clusters.

sections.title: Ich-Laut & Ach-Laut
sections.desc: Two distinct CH sounds

items.title: Ich-Laut /ç/
items.desc: After front vowels (e, i, ö, ü) and consonants. A soft, hissing sound from the palate.

items.title: Ach-Laut /x/
items.desc: After back vowels (a, o, u, au). Deeper, throatier — similar to Dutch G.

items.title: Auslautverhärtung
items.desc: Voiced consonants (b, d, g) become voiceless (p, t, k) at the end of words or syllables.

sections.title: Stress Patterns
sections.desc: Where to put emphasis

items.title: Default: first syllable
items.desc: Most native German words stress the first syllable, just like Dutch.

items.title: Inseparable prefixes
items.desc: be-, ge-, er-, ver-, zer-, ent-, emp- are NEVER stressed.

items.title: Separable prefixes
items.desc: Unlike inseparable ones, separable prefixes (an-, auf-, aus-, ein-, mit-, etc.) ARE stressed.

sections.title: 0.1 Reading Direction & Letter Shapes
sections.desc: How Arabic text flows and how letters change shape

items.title: Right-to-left navigation
items.desc: Arabic text runs right-to-left. Words start at the right margin. Numbers and Latin text inside Arabic lines display left-to-right.

cells.rom: digits read left to right even in Arabic
Arabic books open from the right
English books open from the left

items.title: Arabic is cursive by default
items.desc: Letters connect within words. Each letter takes a different shape depending on its position: isolated, initial, medial, or final.

items.title: Dots are part of letters
items.desc: Many Arabic letters share the same base shape — dots are what distinguish them. Dots are letter IDENTITY, not optional decoration.

sections.title: 0.2 Breakers, Punctuation & Policy
sections.desc: The 6 non-connecting letters, Arabic punctuation, and romanization rules

items.title: The 6 breakers (non-connectors)
items.desc: 6 letters connect ONLY to the preceding letter (right side) and NEVER to the following letter (left side). They create a visible break inside words.

items.title: Arabic punctuation
items.desc: Arabic uses mirrored punctuation marks placed in RTL context.

items.title: Romanization policy
items.desc: There is no single universal way to write Arabic in Latin letters. Romanization is OFF by default — used only as optional scaffolding, revealed after attempts, never in mastery gates.

sections.title: 1.1 Teeth Base & Core Shapes
sections.desc: The ب/ت/ث family, ن/ي, alif, and the anchor letters ل/م

items.title: Family: ب ت ث (dots on teeth base)
items.desc: Same base shape — distinguished by dot count and placement. High-frequency family, essential to master first.

items.title: Family: ن ي (dot/tail pattern)
items.desc: ن has 1 dot above; ي has 2 dots below. Visually confusable in some positions — focus on dot placement.

items.title: ا alif — breaker + long vowel
items.desc: ا is one of the 6 breakers: it NEVER connects to the following letter. It also serves as a long vowel letter (/aː/).

items.title: ل lam & م mim — anchor letters
items.desc: Two high-frequency joiners. ل is tall and forms important combinations (including with ا). م has a distinctive loop.

sections.title: 1.2 Kaf & Breaker Pairs
sections.desc: ك kaf, plus the breaker pairs د/ذ and ر/ز

items.title: ك kaf — high-utility consonant
items.desc: ك (kaf) is one of the most common Arabic consonants. It connects on both sides and has 4 positional forms.

items.title: Breaker pair: د dal / ذ dhal
items.desc: Same base shape — ذ adds 1 dot above. Both are breakers (only 2 forms: isolated + final).

items.title: Breaker pair: ر ra / ز zay
items.desc: Same base shape — ز adds 1 dot above. Both are breakers.

sections.title: 1.3 Teeth, Ha & Waw
sections.desc: The س/ش teeth family, the shape-shifting ه, and و waw

items.title: Teeth family: س sin / ش shin
items.desc: Same teeth base — ش adds 3 dots above. Common and must be read quickly.

items.title: ه ha — multiple print forms
items.desc: ه can look very different across positions and fonts. Focus on joining points, not exact stroke style.

items.title: و waw — breaker + long vowel
items.desc: و is one of the 6 breakers AND a long vowel letter (/uː/). No left connection.

sections.title: 2.1 Remaining Letter Families
sections.desc: Bowl ج/ح/خ, heavy teeth ص/ض, tall ط/ظ, throat ع/غ, and cup ف/ق

items.title: Bowl family: ج ح خ
items.desc: Same bowl-shaped base — distinguished by dot position. ج = 1 dot below, ح = no dot, خ = 1 dot above.

items.title: Heavy teeth: ص sad / ض dad
items.desc: Same base — ض adds 1 dot above. Do not confuse with the س/ش teeth family.

items.title: Tall loop: ط ta / ظ dha
items.desc: Same tall base — ظ adds 1 dot above.

items.title: Throat loop: ع ayn / غ ghayn
items.desc: Same base — غ adds 1 dot above. High-impact for reading.

items.title: Dot-count pair: ف fa / ق qaf
items.desc: ف has 1 dot above; ق has 2 dots above. Visually related in some fonts.

sections.title: 2.2 Full Reference & Look-alikes
sections.desc: Complete 28-letter table, confusable pairs, and breaker summary

items.title: Full 28-letter reference
items.desc: Every Arabic letter in all 4 positions: Isolated (alone), Initial (start of word), Medial (middle), Final (end of word). Letters marked ★ are breakers — they only have 2 forms.

items.title: Confusable dot clusters
items.desc: Systematic dot discrimination across shape families. Most reading errors come from dot confusion.

items.title: All 6 breakers — consolidation
items.desc: One lookup: the complete breaker rule. After any of these letters, the next letter takes isolated or initial form.

cells.ch: ـا  ـد  ـذ  ـر  ـز  ـو

cells.rom: gap inside a word ≠ word boundary

sections.title: 3.1 Short Vowels & Sukun
sections.desc: The 3 vowel marks (fatḥa, ḍamma, kasra), the silence mark, and shadda

items.title: Why vowels disappear in Arabic
items.desc: Arabic is an abjad: a writing system that mainly writes consonants. Short vowels are shown with small marks called harakat. Beginner texts include them, but newspapers and books usually leave them off — readers fill in the vowels from context.

cells.rom: learner texts show all vowel marks
newspapers and books leave them off

items.title: The 3 short vowel marks
items.desc: Fatha = short /a/ (above), damma = short /u/ (above), kasra = short /i/ (below). Placed on the consonant they follow.

items.title: Sukun — the silence mark
items.desc: Sukun is a small circle placed above a letter. It means: no vowel after this consonant. Compare بَ (ba — has a vowel) with بْ (just "b" — silent, no vowel follows).

items.title: Shadda — doubled consonant
items.desc: Shadda is a small w-shape placed above a letter. It means you say that consonant twice — for example, بَّ sounds like "bb" instead of just "b".

sections.title: 3.2 Tanwin, Long Vowels & Syllables
sections.desc: The -n endings, long vowel letters, and reading your first syllables

items.title: Tanwin — the -n endings
items.desc: Tanwin adds an "-n" sound to the end of a word. It appears as doubled vowel marks. You will see it in fully voweled Arabic texts like the Quran and textbooks.

items.title: Long vowels with ا و ي
items.desc: Three letters can function as long vowel carriers: ا = /aː/, و = /uː/, ي = /iː/. They are LETTERS, not diacritics.

items.title: Reading your first syllables
items.desc: Start by combining one consonant with a vowel mark (open syllable, like بَ = ba). Then try consonant + vowel + consonant with sukun (closed syllable, like بَتْ = bat).

sections.title: 4.1 The & Combos
sections.desc: The definite article ال, the lam-alif combo, and Arabic-Indic digits

items.title: The definite article ال
items.desc: ال (pronounced: al) is Arabic for "the". It is the most common prefix — you will see it at the start of many words.

items.title: Lam-alif combo: لا (laa)
items.desc: When ل (lam) and ا (alif) appear next to each other, they always merge into one combined shape: لا (pronounced: laa). You cannot write them separately — they fuse automatically.

cells.rom: laa — always merge into one shape
whenever you see ل then ا

items.title: Arabic-Indic digits ٠–٩
items.desc: Arabic text can use Arabic-Indic numerals. Digit sequences read left-to-right inside RTL text.

sections.title: 4.2 End-shapes & First Words
sections.desc: Look-alike letters at word endings, plus your first Arabic words to read

items.title: Arabic-Indic digits ٥–٩
items.desc: Continuation of the digit reference.

items.title: Look-alikes: ة vs ه at the end of words
items.desc: ة (called taa marbuuta, sounds like a soft "a" or "t") is extremely common at the end of Arabic words. It looks like ه (ha) but with 2 dots above. Mixing them up changes the word.

items.title: Look-alikes: ى vs ي at the end of words
items.desc: ى (called alif maqsuura, sounds like a long "aa") has NO dots. ي (ya, sounds like "ee") has 2 dots below. They look almost identical — always check for dots underneath.

cells.rom: NO dots — sounds like long aa
2 dots below at end of word

items.title: Your first Arabic words
items.desc: These are some of the shortest, most common Arabic words. Try to read the Arabic letters first, then check the meaning below.

sections.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes & Tips
sections.desc: Collected pitfalls for Arabic script beginners

items.title: Dot confusion
items.desc: The #1 beginner error: missing, misplacing, or miscounting dots.

items.title: Connection mistakes
items.desc: Forcing connections where breaks should be, or breaking where connections should be.

cells.rom: the ا breaks — ر starts fresh
after ا, the ب starts in initial form
ا breaks inside, but still one word

items.title: Harakat vs dots
items.desc: Dots are part of the letter (permanent). Harakat are vowel marks (optional). Do not confuse them.

cells.rom: the dot IS the letter — always required
the mark shows the vowel — optional
dots change which letter you see

sections.title: Hiragana (ひらがな)
sections.desc: 46 base characters for native Japanese words

items.title: Hiragana chart
items.desc: Used for native Japanese words, grammar, and verb endings. Tap any character to see it up close!

items.title: Irregular readings
items.desc: し is 'shi' (not si), ち is 'chi' (not ti), つ is 'tsu' (not tu), ふ is 'fu' (not hu). These are the most common mistakes.

items.title: Dakuten & handakuten
items.desc: Voiced marks (゛) and half-voiced marks (゜) create additional sounds from basic kana.

sections.title: Katakana (カタカナ)
sections.desc: 46 base characters for foreign words & emphasis

items.title: Katakana chart
items.desc: Same sounds as hiragana but angular shapes. Used for loanwords (コーヒー coffee), emphasis, and onomatopoeia.

items.title: Long vowels in katakana
items.desc: A dash ー extends the previous vowel. This is very common in loanwords.

items.title: Japanese pronunciation
items.desc: Japanese has only 5 vowel sounds (a i u e o) and a simple CV syllable structure. Pitch accent (not stress) distinguishes some words. Double consonants (っ) create a pause.
examples: きって kitte (stamp) — っ = pause before t
おばさん obasan (aunt) vs おばあさん obaasan (grandmother) — vowel length matters

items.title: When to use which script
items.desc: Hiragana: native Japanese words + grammar. Katakana: foreign loanwords + emphasis + onomatopoeia. Kanji: Chinese characters for content words. All three scripts mix in every sentence.
examples: 私はコーヒーを飲みます = kanji(私) + hiragana(は) + katakana(コーヒー) + hiragana(を) + kanji(飲) + hiragana(みます)

sections.title: Kanji (漢字) Introduction
sections.desc: Chinese characters used in Japanese — what they are, how they work, and why they matter

items.title: What is kanji?
items.desc: Kanji are Chinese characters adopted into Japanese. Each character represents a meaning (not just a sound). Japanese uses ~2,136 'daily use' kanji (常用漢字). At A1 you'll learn ~50-80. Unlike kana, kanji must be memorized individually — but radicals help.

items.title: On'yomi & Kun'yomi — two reading systems
items.desc: Every kanji has at least two pronunciations. On'yomi (音読み) is the Chinese-derived reading, used in compound words. Kun'yomi (訓読み) is the native Japanese reading, used when the kanji stands alone. This is the single hardest thing about kanji.
examples: 山: on'yomi = サン (san), kun'yomi = やま (yama)
水: on'yomi = スイ (sui), kun'yomi = みず (mizu)

items.title: Basic radicals (部首)
items.desc: Radicals are the building blocks of kanji. Learning ~30 common radicals lets you guess meaning and look up unfamiliar characters. The radical often sits on the left side and hints at the category.

items.title: Kanji + hiragana: how they work together
items.desc: Kanji carry the core meaning. Hiragana endings (okurigana) show grammar — tense, politeness, negation. This is why you NEED hiragana before kanji. Without okurigana, you can't conjugate anything.
examples: 食べる (ta-beru) = eat: 食 = kanji 'eat' + べる = hiragana ending
食べます (ta-bemasu) = eat (polite): same kanji, different ending
食べない (ta-benai) = don't eat: same kanji, negative ending
The kanji 食 stays constant — hiragana changes the grammar

sections.title: Phase 0: The Block System
sections.desc: What is a syllable block? How Korean writing is assembled, not linear.

items.title: How Hangul works
items.desc: Letters combine into syllable blocks. Each block = initial consonant + vowel (+ optional final consonant). The block is read left-to-right, top-to-bottom.

items.title: Block slots: Start + Vowel + (Bottom)
items.desc: Every block has slots: a Start slot (always a consonant), a Vowel zone, and an optional Bottom slot (받침).

sections.title: Phase 1: Core Vowels
sections.desc: 6 base vowels — their shapes determine how blocks are built

items.title: Basic vowels
items.desc: 10 basic vowel letters. Vertical strokes (ㅏ ㅓ ㅣ) go to the right of the consonant, horizontal strokes (ㅗ ㅜ ㅡ) go below.

items.title: The 6 core vowels (learn these first)
items.desc: Start with 6 high-utility vowels. Learn them as contrast pairs: ㅣ vs ㅡ (vertical line vs horizontal line), ㅏ vs ㅓ (stroke right vs left), ㅗ vs ㅜ (stroke above vs below). Vowel shapes are systematic — not random.

items.title: Vertical vs horizontal: the layout rule
items.desc: Vowel direction determines block layout. Vertical vowels sit RIGHT of the consonant. Horizontal vowels sit BELOW the consonant.

items.title: Mirror traps: ㅏ/ㅓ and ㅗ/ㅜ
items.desc: The most common beginner mistake: confusing mirror-image vowels. Always check the direction of the short stroke.

sections.title: Phase 2: Silent ㅇ & First Blocks
sections.desc: The empty starter — how Korean handles vowel-initial syllables

items.title: ㅇ is silent at the start
items.desc: Korean rule: every block MUST begin with a consonant. When the syllable starts with a vowel sound, ㅇ fills the Start slot with NO sound.

items.title: Your first 6 blocks
items.desc: Using ㅇ + each core vowel, you can now read and write 6 real syllable blocks. These are the simplest possible Korean syllables.

sections.title: Phase 3: First Consonants ㄱ ㄴ ㅁ ㅅ
sections.desc: 4 visually distinct consonants — now you can build many real syllables

items.title: ㄱ (g/k)
items.desc: A simple angular shape. One of the most common Korean consonants. Combined with 6 core vowels, you get: 가 거 고 구 그 기. Practice each CV block.

items.title: ㄴ (n)
items.desc: Shaped like an 'L'. Combined with 6 core vowels: 나 너 노 누 느 니. Watch out: ㄱ and ㄴ can look similar at beginner speed.

items.title: ㅁ (m)
items.desc: A square shape. Combined with 6 core vowels: 마 머 모 무 므 미. Watch out: ㅁ (square) and ㅇ (circle) are both 'boxy' — look at the corners.

items.title: ㅅ (s)
items.desc: A hat/tent shape. Combined with 6 core vowels: 사 서 소 수 스 시. Note: 시 sounds closer to 'shi' than 'si' — learn the Korean sound, not the romanization.

sections.title: Phase 4: More Consonants ㄷ ㅂ ㅈ ㅎ
sections.desc: 4 more consonants — still CV blocks only, no batchim yet

items.title: ㄷ (d/t)
items.desc: Combined with 6 core vowels: 다 더 도 두 드 디. Contrast with ㄱ: ㄷ has a flat top.

items.title: ㅂ (b/p)
items.desc: Combined with 6 core vowels: 바 버 보 부 브 비. Watch out: ㅂ vs ㅁ — both look 'boxy' but ㅂ has vertical lines extending down.

items.title: ㅈ (j)
items.desc: Combined with 6 core vowels: 자 저 조 주 즈 지. Watch out: ㅈ vs ㅅ — ㅈ has an extra horizontal stroke on top of the ㅅ shape.

items.title: ㅎ (h)
items.desc: Combined with 6 core vowels: 하 허 호 후 흐 히. ㅎ is distinctive — aim for one clean syllable, don't over-aspirate.

sections.title: Phase 5: The Special ㄹ
sections.desc: ㄹ gets its own phase — it's high-frequency and unfamiliar to most learners

items.title: ㄹ (r/l)
items.desc: ㄹ is its own sound category — don't force it into English 'r' or 'l'. It changes slightly depending on position, but at this stage just recognize the shape and map it to the Korean sound. Combined with 6 core vowels:

items.title: ㄹ vs ㄴ discrimination
items.desc: ㄹ and ㄴ are the most confused consonant pair. Practice minimal pairs.

items.title: Multi-syllable reading
items.desc: Multiple blocks = multiple syllables. Read each block separately, left to right.

sections.title: Phase 6: Batchim Starter Pack
sections.desc: The bottom slot — introducing final consonants with the 4 safest ones first

items.title: What is 받침 (batchim)?
items.desc: 받침 is the final consonant at the bottom of a syllable block. Not every syllable has one.

items.title: Stable batchim set: ㄴ ㅁ ㄹ ㅇ
items.desc: Start with these 4 finals because they behave consistently. ㄴ always sounds [n], ㅁ always sounds [m], ㄹ always sounds [l], ㅇ always sounds [ng]. No surprises — no neutralization yet.

items.title: ㅇ role-switch: silent → ng
items.desc: Same letter, completely different role depending on position.

sections.title: Phase 7: Complex Vowels
sections.desc: 11 compound vowels built systematically from the base vowels you already know

items.title: Compound vowels
items.desc: 11 vowels formed by combining basic vowels.

items.title: Set 7A: ㅐ and ㅔ (spelling distinction)
items.desc: These two vowels sound very similar in modern Korean (both close to 'e'). Different spellings you must recognize.

items.title: Set 7B: ㅑ ㅕ ㅛ ㅠ (y-versions)
items.desc: These are 'y-versions' of base vowels. Visually: they have an EXTRA short stroke compared to the base. ㅑ = ㅏ + extra stroke. ㅕ = ㅓ + extra stroke. ㅛ = ㅗ + extra stroke. ㅠ = ㅜ + extra stroke.

items.title: Set 7C: ㅘ ㅝ ㅚ ㅟ (combinations)
items.desc: Built from ㅗ/ㅜ combined with ㅏ/ㅓ/ㅣ: ㅘ = ㅗ+ㅏ, ㅝ = ㅜ+ㅓ, ㅚ = ㅗ+ㅣ, ㅟ = ㅜ+ㅣ. Same placement rules apply — only the vowel shape changes.

items.title: Set 7D: ㅙ ㅞ ㅢ ㅒ ㅖ (most complex)
items.desc: The densest vowels, saved for last. Rare but you must recognize them.

sections.title: 8.1 All Consonant Types
sections.desc: Basic, aspirated, and tense consonants — the complete set

items.title: All 14 basic consonants
items.desc: 14 basic consonant letters (자음). The shapes were designed to represent tongue/mouth position.

items.title: Aspirated consonants: add a stroke
items.desc: 4 aspirated consonants are made by adding an extra stroke to a plain consonant. This is a visual pattern, not random memorization: ㄱ→ㅋ, ㄷ→ㅌ, ㅂ→ㅍ, ㅈ→ㅊ.

items.title: Tense consonants: double the letter
items.desc: 5 tense/double consonants made by doubling a basic consonant. They have a stronger, tenser sound — distinct categories, not 'just louder.'

sections.title: 8.2 Batchim Rules
sections.desc: The 7 final sounds, batchim in real words, and liaison

items.title: The 7 final sounds (batchim neutralization)
items.desc: Only 7 sounds are possible as batchim, regardless of which consonant is written. Multiple consonants merge into the same final sound. This is the single most important Korean pronunciation rule.

cells.ch: ㄷ ㅌ ㅅ ㅆ ㅈ ㅊ

items.title: Batchim in real words
items.desc: Different written consonants produce the same final sound (neutralization).

items.title: Sound rule 1: Liaison (연음)
items.desc: When batchim meets ㅇ-initial next syllable, the consonant sound moves forward.

sections.title: 8.3 Sound Changes
sections.desc: Nasalization, tensing, and the full sound change summary

items.title: Sound rule 2: Nasalization
items.desc: Before ㄴ/ㅁ, stop consonants become nasal: ㄱ→ㅇ, ㄷ→ㄴ, ㅂ→ㅁ.

items.title: Korean sound changes (summary)
items.desc: All major sound rules in one reference table.

items.title: Sound rule 3: Tensing & other rules (recognition only)
items.desc: Additional sound changes to RECOGNIZE, not memorize.

sections.title: Reference: Syllable Block Assembly
sections.desc: Quick reference for block-building patterns

items.title: Syllable block assembly
items.desc: Every Korean syllable block follows one of these patterns.

cells.rom: ㄱ left, ㅏ right, ㄴ bottom
ㄱ top, ㅗ mid, ㄴ bottom

sections.title: ⚠️ Common Mistakes & Tips
sections.desc: Collected pitfalls from each learning phase — reference when stuck

items.title: Blocks ≠ single letters
items.desc: Each block is ASSEMBLED from parts. □ = one syllable, □□ = two syllables (not two letters). Don't treat 한 as one symbol — it's ㅎ + ㅏ + ㄴ.

items.title: ㅇ mistakes
items.desc: The #1 beginner trap: ㅇ at the start is SILENT — never say 'ng'. And every syllable needs ㅇ in the start slot if no consonant.

items.title: Look-alike consonants
items.desc: The most confused pairs. Focus on the defining visual feature.

sections.title: Pinyin & Tones
sections.desc: Romanization system with 4 tones + neutral

items.title: Pinyin
items.desc: The official romanization of Mandarin. Essential for typing, pronunciation, and dictionary lookup.

items.title: Four Tones
items.desc: Tone 1: high flat (mā), Tone 2: rising (má), Tone 3: dipping (mǎ), Tone 4: falling (mà). Wrong tone = wrong word.

items.title: Tone grid
items.desc: Each syllable carries one of four tones (plus a neutral tone). Here is 'ma' in all tones:

sections.title: Pinyin Initials
sections.desc: 21 consonant sounds that start syllables

items.title: Pinyin initial consonants
items.desc: Mandarin has 21 initials. Some sound very different from English! Pay special attention to: zh/ch/sh (retroflex), z/c/s (dental), and q/x (palatal).

items.title: Tone sandhi & finals
items.desc: Tone 3 + Tone 3 → Tone 2 + Tone 3 (e.g., 你好 nǐhǎo → níhǎo). Finals include -n (front nasal) vs -ng (back nasal), and -ü (only after j/q/x/l/n). Mandarin has ~400 possible syllables (× 4 tones ≈ 1600).
examples: 你好 nǐhǎo → actually pronounced níhǎo — tone 3 sandhi
不 bù changes to bú before tone 4: 不是 búshì
-an /an/ vs -ang /aŋ/: 三 sān vs 桑 sāng

items.title: Radicals & character structure
items.desc: Chinese characters are built from ~214 radicals (building blocks). The radical often hints at meaning: 氵(water) appears in 河 (river), 海 (sea), 洗 (wash). Knowing radicals helps guess meaning and look up characters.
examples: 氵(water) → 河 (river), 海 (sea), 洗 (wash)
口 (mouth) → 吃 (eat), 喝 (drink), 唱 (sing)

sections.title: Pinyin Finals (韵母)
sections.desc: The vowel combinations that complete every Mandarin syllable — equally important as initials

items.title: Simple finals
items.desc: 6 basic vowels that can stand alone or combine with initials. These are the building blocks of all other finals. Note: 'ü' only appears after j, q, x, l, n (written as 'u' after j/q/x).

items.title: Compound finals
items.desc: Two or three vowels combined. These are the sounds English speakers find hardest. Pay special attention to: -iu (actually -iou), -ui (actually -uei), and -ün (actually -üen).

items.title: The -n vs -ng trap
items.desc: The difference between -n (tongue touches front teeth) and -ng (tongue stays back, nasal) is the #1 pronunciation mistake for English speakers. Mixing them up changes the word entirely.
examples: 三 sān (three) vs 桑 sāng (mulberry) — different final
新 xīn (new) vs 星 xīng (star) — n vs ng
ban vs bang, pin vs ping, min vs ming — always distinct

items.title: Stroke order basics
items.desc: Characters must be written in a specific stroke order. The rules are consistent: left before right, top before bottom, outside before inside, horizontal before vertical. Correct stroke order makes characters look right and enables handwriting recognition.
examples: 十 (ten) = 2 strokes: horizontal first, then vertical

sections.title: The French Alphabet
sections.desc: 26 letters plus accent marks: é è ê ë, à â, ù û, ô, î ï, ç. Accents change pronunciation!

items.title: French letter grid
items.desc: Same 26 Latin letters as English — but French adds accent marks and has very different pronunciation rules.

items.title: French accents
items.desc: é (acute) = /e/, è (grave) = /ɛ/, ê (circumflex) = /ɛ/, ë (diaeresis) = separate vowel, ç (cedilla) = /s/ before a/o/u.

items.title: Nasal vowels
items.desc: French has nasal vowels not found in English: an/en = /ɑ̃/, on = /ɔ̃/, in/ain = /ɛ̃/, un = /œ̃/. The 'n' is not pronounced — it nasalizes the vowel.

items.title: Key French sounds
items.desc: R is uvular (/ʁ/), U is rounded (/y/, like German ü), silent final consonants are the norm. Liaison: a normally-silent final consonant is pronounced when the next word starts with a vowel.

items.title: Silent letters & spelling patterns
items.desc: Most final consonants are silent (except C, R, F, L — 'CaReFuL'). E at the end of a word is usually silent. Double letters rarely change pronunciation. H is always silent.
examples: belle /bɛl/ — e silent, l pronounced (CaReFuL rule)

sections.title: The English Alphabet
sections.desc: 26 letters — but English spelling is famously irregular. The same letter can make many sounds!

items.title: English letter grid
items.desc: Standard 26-letter Latin alphabet. English has no accent marks but compensates with complex spelling rules.

items.title: Spelling traps
items.desc: English spelling is not phonetic: 'ough' has 7+ pronunciations (though, through, thought, tough, cough, bough, hiccough).

items.title: Vowel sounds & stress
items.desc: English has ~15 vowel sounds but only 5 vowel letters. Stress placement changes meaning: 'REcord' (noun) vs 'reCORD' (verb). Schwa /ə/ is the most common vowel sound.

items.title: Common spelling patterns
items.desc: English has silent letters (knife, write, psychology), doubled consonants that show short vowels (hopping vs hoping), and -tion/-sion suffixes pronounced /ʃən/.

sections.title: El Alfabeto Español
sections.desc: 27 letters: the 26 Latin letters plus Ñ. Spanish spelling is very regular: what you see is what you say!

items.title: Spanish letter grid
items.desc: Nearly perfect phonetic spelling. Every letter has a consistent sound. Ñ (/ɲ/) is its own letter, not an accent on N.

items.title: Spanish accents & punctuation
items.desc: Accent marks (á é í ó ú) show stress, not different sounds. ¿ and ¡ open questions and exclamations. Ü (güe/güi) means pronounce the U.

items.title: Key Spanish sounds
items.desc: Spanish has only 5 pure vowel sounds (a e i o u), very consistent. RR is a trill. J is /x/ (like Scottish 'loch'). H is always silent. B and V sound identical.

items.title: Spanish spelling rules
items.desc: Spelling is almost perfectly phonetic. C before e/i = /θ/ (Spain) or /s/ (Latin America). G before e/i = /x/. QU before e/i = /k/. LL = /ʎ/ or /ʝ/.

sections.title: Alfabetul Românesc
sections.desc: 31 letters: 26 Latin letters plus 5 special characters: Ă Â Î Ș Ț. Romanian spelling is very regular.

items.title: Romanian letter grid
items.desc: Romanian uses 5 extra characters: Ă (schwa), Â/Î (central close vowel), Ș (sh), Ț (ts). Spelling is phonetic.

items.title: Special Romanian letters
items.desc: Ă = schwa (like 'a' in 'about'), Â/Î = same sound (no English equivalent), Ș = 'sh', Ț = 'ts'. Â is used inside words, Î at the start/end.

items.title: Romanian pronunciation
items.desc: Romanian is highly phonetic. Every letter has a consistent sound. Stress varies but is often on the penultimate syllable. The definite article is suffixed, not prefixed.

items.title: Romanian spelling patterns
items.desc: CE/CI = /tʃe/, /tʃi/. GE/GI = /dʒe/, /dʒi/. CHE/CHI = /ke/, /ki/. GHE/GHI = /ge/, /gi/. These digraph rules are 100% consistent.

sections.title: The Russian Alphabet (Кириллица)
sections.desc: 33 letters: 10 vowels, 21 consonants, plus the soft sign (ь) and hard sign (ъ).

items.title: Cyrillic letter grid
items.desc: Many letters look like Latin but sound different! А=A, В=V (not B!), Н=N (not H!), Р=R (not P!), С=S (not C!).

items.title: False friends (look-alike traps)
items.desc: These Cyrillic letters look Latin but sound completely different. This is the #1 beginner mistake!

items.title: Soft sign (ь) & Hard sign (ъ)
items.desc: ь softens the preceding consonant (like adding a tiny 'y'). ъ separates a consonant from a following vowel. Neither makes a sound on its own.
examples: объект /ɐbʲˈjekt/ (object): hard sign separates Б from Е

items.title: Russian vowel reduction & stress
items.desc: Unstressed О sounds like /ɐ/ (close to A). Unstressed Е/Я become /ɪ/. Stress is unpredictable and must be memorized per word. This vowel reduction is the #2 challenge after Cyrillic.
examples: молоко /mɐlɐˈko/ (milk): the two О's before stress = /ɐ/

items.title: Russian spelling rules
items.desc: After К Г Х Ж Ч Ш Щ, never write Ы (use И). After Ж Ч Ш Щ Ц, never write Ю/Я (use У/А). These 'spelling rules' are 100% consistent and simplify writing.
